session_1.text: Scene 6c: Explanations

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: Sorry, guys. John, please continue.

session_1.text: Come on, I thought we were done with that.

session_1.speaker: Harry

session_1.text: Besides, I think he's making a certain amount of sense.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Like Hegel: logic from absurd premises.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: That Van Gogh?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: He gave it to me. I was, uh, Jacque Borne at the time.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: A pig farmer.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: A pig farmer?

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Harry and Art Laugh, the ladies giggle/smile.

session_1.text: I like to work with my hands. He would come out to the place, paint. We talked about capturing nature in Art. Turner, Cezanne, Pissarro.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Oh, the Nolde landscapes.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Not in Van Gogh's time. He would have loved them, though.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Yes.

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Well, I don't understand why you can't remember where you're from. Geography hasn't changed. I learned that in-

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Professor Hansen’s tepid lectures. But you're right.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Where did you live when you were five years old?

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Little Rock.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Your mother, she took you to the market?

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Mm-hmm.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: What direction was it? From your house.

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: I-I don't know.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: How far?

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Um, three blocks.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Were there any references that stuck in your mind?

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Well, there was a gas station and a big field. I was told I could never go there alone.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: And if you went back there today, would it be the same?

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: No. I'm sure it's all different and built up.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Thus the saying: "You can't go home again."

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Because it isn't there anymore. Picture it on my scale- I migrated through an endless flat space full of endless new things- Forests, mountains, tundra, canyons. My memory sees what I saw then.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: My eye sees highways, flyovers, urban sprawl, Big Macs under the Eiffel tower.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Early on, the world got bigger and bigger, and then...

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Think what I've had to unlearn.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: And now you're moving on.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: As you've said, there's talk of my not aging, and when that happens, I move on.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Well, it might make sense to set up your next identity, your next ten years, and then just drop into it.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I've done that a few times, even passed as my own son. "Oh, you're an engineer, too? You're Ben's son. He was a good man." Saves trouble with credentials and references.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: On the other hand, I've been busted a few times.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Spent a year in jail, Belgium, 1862- I won't forget that- For faking a government application.

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: When'd you come to America?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: 1890, right after Van Gogh's death, with some French immigrants...

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Moving On.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Pause.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: An answer for every question. Except one, John.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Why're you doing this?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: A whim. Maybe not such a good idea. I... wanted to say goodbye to you as me, not what you thought I was.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Well, since this isn't funny, we think you might have a problem. A very serious problem.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I've got boxes to move.

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: I'll give you a hand.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Wouldn't you have some relic, an artifact to remind you of your early life? Like this, maybe?

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: (holds up the burin)

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Thrift shop. Really.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: If you lived a hundred- a thousand years...

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: (holding up a pen)

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Would you still have this? What would cause you to keep it? As a memento to your beginnings, even if you didn't have the concept of beginnings? It would be gone, lost.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: No. I don't have artifacts.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: (tosses the pen to Harry)

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: (to Dan)

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Keep that.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Interesting. You could have lied about that.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Don't talk about me while I'm gone.
Scene 7: Sandy
John and Sandy load a few boxes. Sandy pauses

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: I love you, you know.

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: Soft music.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I know.

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: Since my first week at the office.

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: ...And?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I care very much about you, but now you know what you'd be getting into.

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: Do you really think you're a caveman?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Do you?

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: Could you love me, or don't you believe in that anymore?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I've gotten over it too many times.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Fond of you... Certainly attracted to you.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Sandy giggles.

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: That's it?

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: ...I can work with that.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: If what I'm saying is true, you and any children will age. I won't. And one day I'll leave.

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: You'll go back to your May-December romances.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: The simple fact is that I can't give you forever.

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: How long's forever? Who ever really has it?

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: My parents split up before I was born, and then my mom's next marriage lasted what, a whole three years?

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: Then there's death, illness, acts of god...no one knows how long they have. Or how little. I love you. Take whatever you can get.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Like ten years?
Scene 8: Cave-paintings and Buddha

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Is he serious?

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Art walks over to the phone.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: If he is, I'm sorry to say he's...

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Oh, how could he have concealed that for ten years?

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: At least he doesn't appear to be dangerous.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: What are you doing?

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Checkin' for a hidden mic. Candid Camera.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Art is on the phone to one side.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: He's fabricating these wild stories. I've never seen him acting like this.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Oh, it's crazy.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Alright. Alright, a-as soon as you can, then.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: As John enters the room, Harry jumps him, yelling.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Ah! Ha ha ha!

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: John expertly counters, and pushes him to the floor.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Uhn! Jeez! Oh.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Why did you do that?

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: I wanted to see how fast you were. Check your reflexes.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I don't have eyes in the back of my head, I can't hear a flea walking, I am not in any way superman.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Yeah. Well, I'm a second-degree black belt.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Give it another thousand years.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: (Gives him a hand)

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Well. I got it, I got it, I got it.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: (Struggling to his feet)

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Oh. Jesus.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Smooth demonstration, Harry.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Sit on it, Dan.

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: I still have questions.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Ya, I-I do too, John. I mean, a-a-are we done with prehistory yet? Remember any of your original language?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: A little. One thing hasn't changed much...

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: (wolf whistles towards Sandy, causing her to blush)

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Did you ever do any cave Art?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Do you know the rock Art at Les Eyzies?

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Mm-hmm?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: It was the work of a man named... Giraud. He did a pretty good job. He would draw the animals that we hoped to find to eat. One day after a fruitless hunt, our chief stomped his teeth out because his magic had failed him. After that, someone had to chew his food for him. Finally, he got- I suspect- An infected jaw, and he was abandoned.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: That's awful.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: You have to know what to kill.

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Is this why all your students say your knowledge of history is...so amazing?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: No, that's mostly based on study. Remember, it's one man, one place at a time, my solitary viewpoint of a world I knew almost nothing about.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Well, let's talk about what you say you do know about- Historical times.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Don't encourage him.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Edith.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Next few thousand years, it got warmer.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: A few thousand years- See, now, I know you're guessing.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: You can't get there from here, Art.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Well then, pray, continue.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: We hunted reindeer, mammoths-

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: ...Bison, horses. The game retreated northward as the climate changed, you got the idea of growing food rather than gathering it, raising animals rather than hunting them. Am- Am I- Am I getting warm, here? I bet I am. Lakeside living becomes commonplace! Fishing, fowling- Come on!

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: John, this is out of any textbook.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Even yours. You got most of it right. Eventually I headed to the East. I'd grown curious about the world. I'd gotten the hang of going it alone, learning how to fit in when I wanted to.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: East. Towards the rising sun?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Yes. I thought it might be warmer there. That's when I saw an ocean. The Mediterranean, probably. It was around the beginning of the Bronze Age, so I followed the trade routes from the East. Copper. Tin. Learning languages as I went. Everywhere, creation myths, new gods, so many, so different. I finally realized that it was...probably all hogwash.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: So I was Sumerian for 2,000 years, then finally Babylonian under Hammurabi. Great man. And I sailed as a Phoenician for a time.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: See, moving on had been easier as a Hunter- Gatherer...difficult when villages emerged.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Tougher still in city states where authority was centralized. Strangers were suspect. It seemed as though I was always moving on.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I learned some new tricks- even faked my death a couple of times.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I continued east to India, luckily at the time of Gautama Buddha.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Luckily.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Most extraordinary man I've ever known. He taught me things I'd never thought about before.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: You studied... with the Buddha?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Until he died. He knew there was something different about me. I never told him.
